flashcards.back: A complete path starting from the root directory (/). It provides the full location of a file or directory from the system's root. Example: /home/user/documents/file.txt
A path relative to the current working directory. It does not start from root. Example: If in /home/user, ../admin means /home/admin.
Print Working Directory - displays the absolute path of the current directory you are in.
Change Directory - used to navigate between directories. Usage: cd [directory_path]

flashcards.front: What does '.' (single dot) represent in paths?
flashcards.back: Represents the current directory. Used in relative paths. Example: ./script.sh executes script.sh in current directory.

flashcards.front: What does '..' (double dot) represent in paths?
flashcards.back: Represents the parent directory (one level up). Used to move up in the directory hierarchy. Example: cd .. moves to parent directory.
Represents the home directory of the current user. Example: cd ~ takes you to /home/username.

flashcards.front: What does '-' (hyphen) represent in cd command?
flashcards.back: Represents the previous working directory. cd - switches back to the directory you were in before the last cd command.
The top-level directory in the file system hierarchy, denoted by '/'. All other directories branch from here.
List - displays the contents (files and directories) of the current directory or specified directory.
Make Directory - creates new directories. Usage: mkdir [options] directory_name.
Remove Directory - removes empty directories. Usage: rmdir directory_name.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between absolute and relative paths in terms of starting point?
flashcards.back: Absolute paths always start from root (/), while relative paths start from the current working directory.

flashcards.front: How do you navigate to the parent of parent directory?
flashcards.back: Use 'cd ../..' - this moves up two levels in the directory hierarchy.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of the 'cd .' command?
flashcards.back: It stays in the current directory (no change). It's mainly used in scripts and for path construction purposes.
